title: Your first method
successMessage: One method, three calls. Change the format in greet() once and all three outputs update. This is why methods exist — eliminate duplication, centralise logic.

title: Parameters and return values
instruction:
Methods can take multiple parameters and return any type.

Write a `bmi(double weight, double height)` method that returns weight / (height * height).
hint: static double bmi(double weight, double height) { return weight / (height * height); }

codeChecks.message: Define: static double bmi(double weight, double height)

successMessage: 22.9 and 27.8 — correct BMI values. The method takes two doubles, does the math, and returns a double. printf with %.1f formats to one decimal place.

title: Method overloading
successMessage: Same name, three different implementations. Java picks based on argument types and count. Overloading is how print() in many libraries works — it accepts any type.

title: Varargs — variable argument count
hint: static int sum(int... nums) { int total = 0; for (int n : nums) total += n; return total; }
successMessage: Varargs turns the arguments into an array — iterate with for-each. Java's own System.out.printf uses varargs: printf(format, arg1, arg2, ...) — any number of args.

title: Early return
hint: for (int i = 2; i * i <= n; i++) { if (n % i == 0) return false; } return true;

codeChecks.message: Return false when a factor is found.
Return true at the end (no factors found).

successMessage: i * i <= n is the key optimisation — you only need to check up to the square root. If no factor found up to √n, n is prime. Multiple returns make the logic clean.

title: Recursion — methods calling themselves
instruction:
A method that calls itself is recursive. Every recursive method needs:
1. **Base case** — stops the recursion
2. **Recursive case** — calls itself with a smaller problem

`factorial(5) = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120`
successMessage: Factorial: 10! = 3,628,800. Fibonacci: each number is the sum of the two before it. Recursion is elegant — but for large n, iteration is usually faster and safer (no stack overflow).

title: Build a string utilities class
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Write three static utility methods:
- `static boolean isPalindrome(String s)` — returns true if s is the same forwards and backwards (case-insensitive)
- `static int countVowels(String s)` — count a, e, i, o, u
- `static String titleCase(String sentence)` — first letter of each word capitalised

Test each method.
hint: For isPalindrome: reverse with StringBuilder and compare. For titleCase: split on spaces, capitalize each word.
successMessage: Three utility methods — reusable, testable, and clean. This is how Java utility classes are built in practice. Chapter 8 done!
